title: Fungal nail follow-up
source_review_basis: full authoritative documents inspected and archived; item-level retain/rewrite/remove/add comparisons recorded

assessment.text: Supports ringworm, tinea, dermatophyte, circular scaly rash, body-site terminology, spread, person, animal, and contaminated-surface exposure context.
assessment.rationale: The CDC healthcare-professional overview identifies onychomycosis as fungal nail infection and supports documenting affected nail site, interval course, foot involvement, contact or exposure context, and diabetes risk without confirming fungal cause.

source.title: Clinical Overview of Ringworm
source.population: People with suspected ringworm or dermatophyte infection, including skin, scalp, foot, groin, and nail presentations.
source.setting: Clinical recognition, exposure assessment, and diagnostic testing for suspected fungal skin or nail infection.
source.exclusions: Partial for selected tinea-corporis identity, exposure, lesion-feature, extent, and testing documentation. It does not support UAE pathways, autonomous diagnosis, medicine, dose, treatment, referral urgency, or fixed follow-up.

exact_location.heading: At a glance, overview, types, and transmission
exact_location.locator: official page sections “At a glance” through “How it spreads”

source.evidence_paraphrase: Supports ringworm, tinea, dermatophyte, circular scaly rash, body-site terminology, spread, person, animal, and contaminated-surface exposure context.

assessment.text: Supports erythematous or differently pigmented raised scaly rings, central clearing, multiple or extensive lesions, and exudative lesions when bacterial superinfection has developed.
assessment.rationale: The clinical-features section supports clinician-observed discoloration, thickening, fragility, cracking, nail separation, pain, foot fungal features, secondary infection, and immune or diabetes context; generic examination and concern fields remain conditional on assessment.

source.title: Clinical Overview of Ringworm
source.population: People with suspected ringworm or dermatophyte infection, including skin, scalp, foot, groin, and nail presentations.
source.setting: Clinical recognition, exposure assessment, and diagnostic testing for suspected fungal skin or nail infection.
source.exclusions: Partial for selected tinea-corporis identity, exposure, lesion-feature, extent, and testing documentation. It does not support UAE pathways, autonomous diagnosis, medicine, dose, treatment, referral urgency, or fixed follow-up.
source.evidence_paraphrase: Supports erythematous or differently pigmented raised scaly rings, central clearing, multiple or extensive lesions, and exudative lesions when bacterial superinfection has developed.

assessment.text: Supports clinician-selected diagnostic testing when suspected ringworm may be confused with eczema, psoriasis, or another skin condition; no treatment content is mapped.
assessment.rationale: The testing section supports recording already reviewed diagnostic or laboratory results because nail abnormalities have multiple causes; it does not order testing or treatment.
